q: How many cost centres does a large corporate healthcare group manage, and what problem does that create for staff?
a: Roughly 300 cost centres — staff face coding dropdowns too long to navigate accurately, and even the finance exec struggles to explain how site budgets roll up into one file.

q: Why does a dental group's acquisition strategy create an invisible layer of spend?
a: Newly acquired clinics keep their old legacy entity and cards instead of switching to the group's brand, so a second layer of spend sits underneath what head office can actually see.

q: Why has an 8-practice GP group deliberately NOT consolidated its three legal entities into one Xero file?
a: Because the complexity of merging felt worse than the complexity of staying separate — so any group-level view has to be stitched together by hand across separate files and bank feeds.

q: What does it take to issue a new card at one rural/regional health group, and what's the knock-on effect?
a: The CEO has to physically fly to a bank branch to co-sign it — so painful that the business has largely stopped issuing new cards, leaving leavers' cards still active.

q: What's the 'official' workaround for covering a staff member's card during maternity leave at a large corporate healthcare group?
a: Handing the physical card and PIN to a colleague for a few months — sharing credentials because there's no fast way to issue a short-term card.

q: At a 45-clinic radiology group, cards are meant to be one-per-clinic. What actually happens in practice?
a: An area or ops manager covering multiple sites can end up holding 5-6 cards at once — one login controlling several sites' spend.

q: What is the entire 'approval' workflow for receipts at a national radiology group?
a: Emailing a photo of the receipt with the manager CC'd as the approval, then finance manually ticking a 'substantiated' box in a spreadsheet.

q: What's the transaction volume and documentation gap between a clean and a messy clinic inside the same dental group?
a: One clinic runs 15-20 clean transactions a month; another runs 50-60 with barely any support, mixing capex with personal-looking purchases.

q: What feature cluster made an incoming CFO at an 8-practice GP group say 'that's what I want'?
a: Auto-coding, AI-suggested categories, automatic GST extraction, and a card-blocking feature that locks a card until a receipt is provided.

q: How did one finance leader at a radiology group describe their approval process?
a: 'It doesn't really exist' — the only mechanism is CC'ing an external approver into an email.

q: What phrase did a rural/regional health group's CFO use to describe their AP approvals, and why?
a: 'Blanket bullshit approvals' — invoices get rubber-stamped in a shared inbox with no real scrutiny and no real audit trail.

q: What approval gap exists at a dental group between supplier invoices and clinic card spend?
a: Dual approval exists for supplier invoices (practice level, then the controller above a threshold) — but there's no equivalent approval step at all for card or petty-cash spend.

q: How long did month-end used to take at a rural/regional health group?
a: Around the 20th of the following month — by the CFO's own admission, the numbers were barely relevant by the time they landed.

q: How much finance time does a large corporate healthcare group lose each month just on coding for one business unit, even using a free bank tool?
a: Roughly 3 finance-days a month — for one business unit out of many.

q: How does a 5-person finance team keep up with 45 clinics at a national radiology group?
a: They don't scale it — they reconcile everything through manual spreadsheet exports and pivot tables, so every new clinic is pure linear overhead on the same five people.
